summary: Cantrill defends and expands his earlier economic analysis of software, arguing that software has near-zero variable costs and doesn't wear out, which creates unique market dynamics. He uses the Firebird database story as a case study showing how proprietary vendors who get too greedy with pricing push customers past their 'FYO point' (threshold to switch), while open source alternatives benefit from both supply-side contributions and demand-side adoption. His core prediction is that open source will inevitably drive the switching cost toward zero for many software products, and companies must adapt by using open source to drive complementary goods or face obsolescence.
key_points: Software's variable cost of production is essentially zero - only bandwidth costs apply
Software doesn't wear out; bugs aren't degradation but pre-existing attributes being exposed
Open source creates powerful supply-side economics while demand-side adds features and lowers switching costs
Companies that overplay their hand on pricing push customers past their 'FYO point' to switch
The future belongs to companies that use open source to drive complementary goods
money_quotes: At the risk of stating a tautology, the cost of marketing a product is not a part of the variable cost of making the product; the variable cost is simply the cost of manufacturing one more unit, and (for software at least) that cost is damn near zero.

The software works exactly today as it did twenty years ago – it's as good as new. Some mistake software that stops working as having 'worn out', but this is an incorrect inference: the software itself has not changed; a heretofore unknown attribute has merely been exposed – an attribute that was there all along.

It may take years – and it may even take decades – but open source from the supply-side coupled with energy from the demand-side will ultimately drive the FYO point towards zero for many existing software products.

Companies that can use open source to drive complementary goods will survive and thrive; those that can't will slowly whither away, until one day their software is acquired for pennies-on-the-dollar by some company that just happens to have some complementary goods to sell.
key_insight: Open source economics will inevitably commoditize proprietary software by driving switching costs toward zero, forcing companies to compete on complementary goods rather than software licensing.
